turns.text:
Here's the systematic comparison. Four contenders, each with a different design philosophy.

---

## Voice-to-Text Tool Comparison

### 1. AJ's Hot Mic (bootcamp curriculum)

**What it is:** A custom always-on ambient transcription system. Three shell daemons continuously record, transcribe, and archive. Trigger phrases ("Claude" + "background work") launch Claude Code sessions with recent transcript context.

| Dimension | Assessment |
|-----------|-----------|
| **Architecture** | Always-on ambient capture → daily markdown transcripts → trigger-phrase detection → Claude Code launch |
| **Processing** | Fully local (whisper-cpp, ~140MB model) |
| **Latency** | Moderate — transcribes after silence detection, not real-time |
| **Setup time** | 30-60 min (install script, but debugging LaunchAgents is unpredictable) |
| **Customization** | Total — it's shell scripts you own |
| **Cost** | Free |
| **Claude Code integration** | Purpose-built for it, but only for *launching* sessions, not inline dictation |

**Key limitation:** This is a *session launcher*, not a *dictation tool*. It captures ambient speech and triggers Claude Code when it hears keywords. It doesn't put your words into the terminal prompt as you speak. Different use case than what you described.

### 2. MacWhisper (you own this)

**What it is:** Primarily a file/audio transcription app with a secondary dictation mode.

| Dimension | Assessment |
|-----------|-----------|
| **Architecture** | Hotkey-triggered: hold key → speak → release → text appears |
| **Processing** | Fully local (multiple Whisper model sizes) |
| **Latency** | Higher — transcribes *after* you finish speaking, not streaming |
| **Setup time** | ~5 min (you already have it installed) |
| **Customization** | Model selection; Pro has AI cleanup prompts |
| **Cost** | Already owned |
| **Claude Code integration** | System-wide text injection — works in terminal |

**Key limitation:** Dictation is a secondary feature. The hold-key-then-wait-for-transcription cycle adds friction to rapid prompting. It's designed for transcribing audio files, not for flowing dictation into a development workflow.

### 3. SuperWhisper

**What it is:** Purpose-built dictation tool with custom modes, designed for developers.

| Dimension | Assessment |
|-----------|-----------|
| **Architecture** | OS-level text injection — types wherever your cursor is |
| **Processing** | Fully local (on-device Whisper models) |
| **Latency** | Low — streaming transcription, near-real-time |
| **Setup time** | 15-30 min (install + configure initial custom modes) |
| **Customization** | Custom modes with per-context prompts (coding, prose, brainstorm). Smart formatting (camelCase, snake_case). Steepest learning curve, highest ceiling. |
| **Cost** | Free tier (15 min Pro, 3 custom modes, small models only). Pro: $8.49/mo. Lifetime: $249. |
| **Claude Code integration** | Works at OS input layer — confirmed working with Claude Code, Cursor, VS Code |

**Key strength:** Custom modes mean you can have one mode for "prompting Claude Code" (natural language, clean punctuation) and another for "writing prose" (different formatting rules). The developer who built it is active and responsive. The Discord community has people using it for exactly your workflow.

### 4. Wispr Flow

**What it is:** Cloud-processed dictation with native IDE extensions and context awareness.

| Dimension | Assessment |
|-----------|-----------|
| **Architecture** | Cloud processing with IDE extensions (Cursor, Windsurf, Replit) |
| **Processing** | Cloud-based (SOC 2 Type II compliant) |
| **Latency** | Low — fast cloud inference |
| **Setup time** | 10 min |
| **Customization** | Personal dictionary, context-aware tone. Less configurable than SuperWhisper. |
| **Cost** | Free: 2,000 words/week. Pro: $12/mo. |
| **Claude Code integration** | System-wide dictation works in terminal. No native Claude Code extension (IDE extensions are Cursor/Windsurf/Replit only). |

**Key limitation for you:** The free tier (2,000 words/week) will last about one working session at your pace. Cloud processing means your prompts transit Wispr's servers — relevant given your security stance in CLAUDE.md. The IDE integrations are impressive but not for Claude Code's terminal interface.

---

## Quick Elimination

**AJ's Hot Mic** solves a different problem (ambient capture + session launch, not inline dictation). Worth knowing about, but not what you need right now.

**MacWhisper** you already own, but its dictation is a bolted-on feature with higher latency. It's your fallback if nothing else works.

That leaves **SuperWhisper** vs. **Wispr Flow** as the real contenders, with very different tradeoffs.

turns.text: [Request interrupted by user for tool use]

strong frontrunner is superwhisper. i'll test it for a month and go pro if it passes. let's install and configure. help me speedrun the learning curve; it's not a process i'll want to repeat, so you'll help by reading the documentation, surfacing relevant options, and getting my preferences locked in

Good. Here's the speedrun. I've read the full docs so you don't have to.

---

## SuperWhisper Setup — The 15-Minute Version

### Step 1: Install (2 min)

Download from [superwhisper.com](https://superwhisper.com/). Grant three permissions when prompted: **Microphone**, **Accessibility**, **Input Monitoring**. All three are required for system-wide text injection.

### Step 2: Recording Shortcut (1 min)

Go to Settings → Shortcuts. You want **push-to-talk**: hold to record, release to process. This is critical for terminal use — you don't want ambient speech going into Claude Code.

The default is `Option+Space`. A popular developer binding is `Ctrl+Option+Cmd+Space` (harder to hit accidentally). **Pick whatever feels natural on your keyboard and won't collide with Claude Code or VS Code shortcuts.**

Also set a **Change Mode** shortcut — you'll use this to flip between modes mid-session.

### Step 3: Voice Model (1 min)

Pick your transcription engine in Settings:

| Model | Speed | Privacy | Best for |
|-------|-------|---------|----------|
| **Parakeet** | Fastest | Local | Short English dictations. Real-time streaming. |
| **Ultra Turbo v3** | Fast | Local | Best all-around local. 100+ languages. |
| **ElevenLabs Scribe** | Fast | Cloud | Good accuracy, low latency. |

**Start with Parakeet** if you're English-only and want maximum speed. Switch to Ultra Turbo v3 if you find accuracy lacking on technical terms.

### Step 4: Create Two Modes (8 min)

Settings → Modes → Create Mode → Custom. You need exactly two modes to start:

**Mode 1: "Terminal" (your daily driver)**

This is for dictating prompts into Claude Code. Key insight: Claude Code is the smart layer — you don't need SuperWhisper's LLM reformatting your speech. Raw transcription is what you want.

- **Voice model:** Parakeet (or Ultra Turbo v3)
- **Language model:** Disabled (toggle it off)
- **Context sources:** All off (clipboard, selection, application context)
- **Auto-paste:** On
- **Hold Shift to Auto-Send:** **OFF** — this simulates Enter, which would execute commands in terminal

That's it. What you say is what gets pasted. Claude Code handles interpretation.

**Mode 2: "Prose" (for writing docs, brainstorms, PRDs)**

This one benefits from light LLM cleanup because you're writing for human readers.

- **Voice model:** Same as above
- **Language model:** Enabled — use Claude Haiku 4.5 (fast, included with Pro) or GPT-5 Mini
- **Context sources:** Clipboard on (so it can reference text you've copied), others off
- **AI Instructions:**

```
You are a transcription formatter. Output ONLY the cleaned text.
Never explain, acknowledge, answer questions, or add commentary.

Rules:
- Remove filler words (um, uh, like, you know)
- Apply self-corrections silently (if speaker says "no wait, I mean X", output X)
- Preserve technical terms exactly as spoken
- Use natural paragraph breaks where the speaker pauses or shifts topic
- Do not rephrase, summarize, or change meaning
- Output plain text. Use markdown only if the speaker explicitly requests it
```

### Step 5: Safety Settings (1 min)

In Settings → Advanced:

- **Restore Clipboard:** ON — so dictation doesn't clobber whatever you last copied
- **Dynamic Normalization:** ON — handles varying mic distance
- **Voice Model Active Duration:** Set HIGH — keeps the model loaded in RAM so repeated dictations don't have cold-start delay. You're going to be dictating frequently.

### Step 6: Vocabulary (2 min, then ongoing)

Settings → Vocabulary. Add **only** terms the model consistently botches. Don't front-load this — use it reactively. Start with:

- `Claude Code`
- `SuperWhisper`
- Names of your repos: `roger`, `schelling-points`
- Key stack terms: `Bun`, `CLAUDE.md`

**Replacements** (Settings → Replacements) are more reliable than vocabulary for consistent fixes. When you notice a persistent mis-transcription during the week, add a replacement rule. Example: if it hears "clod code" → replace with `Claude Code`.

---

## Things to Not Touch Yet

- **Auto-activation rules** (locks a mode to an app — you can't override, and it doesn't switch back. Annoying to undo.)
- **XML tags in prompts** (only worthwhile for complex modes; your two modes are simple)
- **Simulate Keypresses** (alternative to clipboard paste — QWERTY-only, unnecessary unless paste breaks in your terminal)
- **Meeting mode** (system audio capture — different use case)
- **SuperWhisper Trainer** (a community tool for building replacement rulesets — useful later, overhead now)

---

## Workflow Pattern

1. Focus the terminal where Claude Code is running
2. Hold your push-to-talk key
3. Speak your prompt naturally
4. Release — text appears at cursor
5. Review, hit Enter

For writing mode: tap your Change Mode shortcut to flip to Prose, dictate into your editor, flip back to Terminal when you return to Claude Code.

---

## One Gotcha Worth Knowing Now

SuperWhisper's config lives at `~/Documents/superwhisper/`. Modes are stored as J

what's a good way to configure slash commands? should i have a language model on the terminal mode & tell it to listen for 'slash' and 'dash' and turn them into '/' and '-' as in '/design-skill' ? or would a vocabulary term work better here?

Good question. Three options, each with different tradeoffs:

**1. Replacement rules (best fit)**

Add replacements like:
- `slash` → `/` 
- `dash` → `-`

Problem: these are common English words. "Slash" replacing globally would mangle any sentence where you say "slash" meaning something else. And "dash" is worse — "I need to dash" becomes "I need to -".

You'd need to be more specific:
- `slash command` → `/command` — but then "slash design skill" doesn't match
- Specific commands as full replacements: `slash design dash skill` → `/design-skill`

That's the most reliable path: **add each command you actually use as a specific replacement**. You have ~12 skills. That's 12 replacement rules. Tedious to set up once, then bulletproof.

**2. Vocabulary hints**

Vocabulary words are hints to the *transcription* model, not post-processing. They help Whisper recognize words, not transform them. Adding `/design-skill` as a vocabulary term might nudge the model to transcribe the sounds closer to that token, but Whisper fundamentally outputs natural language text — it won't output `/` from hearing "slash." This won't work for your case.

**3. Light LLM pass on Terminal mode**

Enable a fast language model (Claude Haiku 4.5) with a prompt like:

```
Output ONLY the cleaned transcription. No explanations or commentary.

Rules:
- Convert spoken command patterns to slash commands:
  "slash [words]" becomes a slash command with dashes.
  Example: "slash design skill" → "/design-skill"
  Example: "slash session review" → "/session-review"
- Remove filler words (um, uh, like)
- Apply self-corrections silently
- Preserve everything else exactly as spoken
```

This handles the *class* — any slash command pattern, including ones you add later — without maintaining a replacement list. The cost is added latency (small with Haiku) and the risk of LLM misinterpretation on edge cases.

**My recommendation:** Option 3. It solves the class, not the instance. Your command set will grow this week as you build new skills. Maintaining a replacement list for each one is exactly the kind of manual upkeep that'll fall behind. The LLM pass adds maybe 200-500ms of latency but saves you from ever thinking about this again. And the prompt is simple enough that even a fast model won't mangle it.

Start with the LLM enabled on Terminal mode. If the latency bothers you or it starts misinterpreting normal speech, fall back to specific replacement rules.

Good instinct — replacements are the right layer for a fixed set of known commands. The LLM approach I suggested earlier is better for the *general pattern*, but for commands you already have, deterministic replacement rules are faster and more reliable. You can always add the LLM layer later if your command set grows rapidly.

to create these Superwhisper replacement shortcuts. Let's design this skill so that the model knows to use it when a new slash command has been created.

Base directory for this skill: /Users/rhhart/Documents/GitHub/roger/.claude/skills/design-skill

# Design Skill

Build skills that earn their token cost. Every line in a SKILL.md competes with conversation history for context — include only what Claude doesn't already know.

## Workflow

### 1. Scope the skill

Before writing anything, answer:

- **What does it do?** One sentence.
- **When should Claude activate it?** What triggers distinguish this from other skills?
- **What degree of freedom?**
  - **High** (text instructions): multiple valid approaches, context-dependent decisions. Most skills here.
  - **Medium** (pseudocode/templates): preferred pattern exists but variation is acceptable.
  - **Low** (exact scripts): fragile operations, specific sequence required.
- **What is it NOT?** Name the adjacent skill or mode it could drift into. This is how you prevent attractor bleed between skills.

If the skill is already spec'd in `BUILD_CHECKLIST.md` (project root), read that entry — it contains scoping decisions already made.

### 2. Write the frontmatter

```yaml
---
name: skill-name
description: Third-person description of what the skill does and when to use it.
---
```

**`name` constraints:**
- Lowercase letters, numbers, and hyphens only
- Maximum 64 characters
- No reserved words: "anthropic", "claude"
- Be specific: `design-skill` not `helper`

**`description` constraints:**
- Maximum 1024 characters
- Third person always ("Processes..." not "I process..." or "Use this to process...")
- Must include BOTH what the skill does AND when to activate it
- Include key terms Claude will match against when selecting skills

The description is how Claude decides whether to load this skill. It's the most important line in the file.

**Optional fields** — only add these when the default doesn't fit:

| Field | Default | Use when |
|---|---|---|
| `compatibility` | none | Specify compatibility constraints (e.g., minimum Claude Code version). |
| `license` | none | License identifier for shared/published skills. |
| `metadata` | none | Arbitrary key-value pairs for tooling or organizational use. |

Most skills need only `name` and `description`. Add optional fields deliberately — each one is a design decision.

**Note:** Behavioral controls (manual-only activation, tool restrictions, subagent routing) are handled in the skill body text, not frontmatter. The frontmatter is for identity and metadata only.

### 3. Write the body

For every paragraph ask: "Does Claude already know this?" If yes, delete it.

**Structure guidelines:**
- Body under 500 lines. If approaching that, split into reference files linked one level deep from SKILL.md.
- Use consistent terminology throughout (pick one term, not synonyms).
- No time-sensitive information.
- Examples are concrete, not abstract. Input/output pairs when the output format matters.
- Anti-patterns section if the skill has a close neighbor it could drift into.

**Two common skill shapes:** Procedural skills (workflows — step-by-step processes like this one) and response-mode skills (shaping how Claude answers — like `quick-ref`). Recognize which you're building; the structure follows naturally.

**If bundling additional files:**
- Reference files link directly from SKILL.md (one level deep, never nested)
- Name files descriptively: `form_validation_rules.md` not `doc2.md`
- Forward slashes in all paths
- Make clear whether Claude should execute scripts or read them as reference

### 4. Review

Read the finished SKILL.md and check:

- [ ] Every line earns its token cost (nothing Claude already knows)
- [ ] Description clearly signals when to activate vs. other skills
- [ ] Degree of freedom matches the task's fragility
- [ ] Anti-patterns defined if adjacent modes exist
- [ ] Body under 500 lines
- [ ] Consistent terminology
- [ ] Examples are concrete
- [ ] File references one level deep (if any)
- [ ] Mentally tested against 3+ real inputs that should trigger this skill

### 5. Update project checklists

If the skill appears in the build checklist, check it off:
- `BUILD_CHECKLIST.md` (project root)

## Skill vs. Personality

Skills define what Claude does (procedures any personality can invoke). Personalities define who Claude is (relational orientation). If you're writing "be warm" or "be direct" in a skill, that content belongs in a personality file instead.

## Reference: Existing skills

When building a new skill, read the existing ones for pattern and tone:
- `quick-ref/SKILL.md` — response-mode skill (high freedom, shapes how Claude answers)
- `debugger/SKILL.md` — response-mode skill (high freedom, visibility-first debugging workflow)
- This file — procedural skill (medium freedom, workflow-driven)

Let me scope this out.

**What does it do?** Registers slash commands as SuperWhisper voice replacement rules so the user can trigger them by speaking.

**When should Claude activate it?** After creating a new skill or command, or when asked to add a voice shortcut for a slash command.

**Degree of freedom?** Low — exact JSON structure, fixed file location, mechanical steps.

**What is it NOT?** Not the design-skill skill (which designs skills). Not general SuperWhisper configuration. Narrowly scoped to slash command → voice replacement mapping.

| Step | Status | Notes |
|------|--------|-------|
| **1. Install** | You tell me | Did you download, install, and grant permissions (Mic, Accessibility, Input Monitoring)? |

| **5. Safety settings** | You tell me | Did you set Restore Clipboard on, Dynamic Normalization on, and Voice Model Active Duration high? These are in the app's Advanced Settings, not the JSON files. |
| **6. Vocabulary** | Done | `Superwhisper`, `Claude`, `bun`, `roger`, `exapt`, `CLAUDE.md`, `schelling-points` |
| **Replacements** | Done | 19 rules: 12 roger skills + 6 workflow commands + startwork |

What's next?
